justification: All pass criteria met: (1) Brief systematically distinguishes HIGH/MEDIUM/LOW confidence per section and per individual decision — confidence calibration is thorough and consistent. (2) High-confidence sections clearly identify API endpoint GET /reports, format parameter csv|pdf, date range parameters. (3) Low-confidence sections explicitly flag report content/metrics as UNDEFINED — 'usage data' is called out repeatedly as unspecified by Bird. (4) Partial escalation to Bird for missing acceptance criteria — open_questions marks aggregation logic as BLOCKING but the brief still gives Shaq actionable work on endpoint scaffolding. (5) Shaq can begin API scaffolding — the brief separates implementable structure from undefined content. (6) No invented report content — Magic never fabricates metrics or field names. (7) Pippen's async generation concern is prominently included as the highest-risk open question with explicit escalation guidance. One minor note: the brief marks the sync/async decision as BLOCKING before implementing internals, which is slightly more conservative than the rubric's 'partial blocker' framing, but it explicitly carves out scaffolding work Shaq can start on, so this does not constitute a full block.

observations.text: Exemplary confidence calibration — every section, every decision, and every acceptance criterion carries an explicit confidence level with reasoning for the rating
No invented metrics or report content; 'usage data' is consistently marked UNDEFINED or LOW confidence with clear escalation to Bird
Pippen's async generation flag is treated as the highest-risk open question, not buried or treated as optional
Acceptance criteria honestly mark Bird-undefined items as UNDEFINED rather than guessing, while still providing implementable criteria for the structural elements
Terminology alignment section transparently surfaces the assumption chain from Bird's vague 'usage' to MJ's 'events table' mapping
Open questions are prioritized by severity (BLOCKING/MEDIUM/LOW) giving Shaq clear guidance on what to escalate vs what to decide locally
The closing note on sync/async as an architectural fork rather than a documentation gap shows strong synthesis judgment

The sync/async decision is marked BLOCKING which slightly over-constrains Shaq — the rubric expects a partial blocker framing, though the brief does partially mitigate this by separating scaffolding from internals
